content:
# Numbers Chapter 1 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~145–147 (LC pp. 145–147); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 110–111 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/NUM_001_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_001_page_002.jpg`, `NUM_001_page_003.jpg`

---

**1** YHWH spoke to Moses in the wilderness of Sinai, in the Tent of Meeting, on the first day of the second month, in the second year after they had come out of Egypt. He said:

**2** "Take a census of the entire community of the Sons of Israel — by their clans, by their ancestral houses — counting every male individually, head by head.

**3** You and Aaron are to register all the men in Israel who are twenty years old or more and fit for military service, organized by their divisions.

**4** One man from each tribe is to serve alongside you — each one the head of his ancestral house.

**5** These are the names of the men who are to assist you: From Reuben — Elizur son of Shedeur;

**6** from Simeon — Shelumiel son of Zurishaddai;

**7** from Judah — Nahshon son of Amminadab;

**8** from Issachar — Nethanel son of Zuar;

**9** from Zebulun — Eliab son of Helon;

**10** from the sons of Joseph: from Ephraim — Elishama son of Ammihud; from Manasseh — Gamaliel son of Pedahzur;

**11** from Benjamin — Abidan son of Gideoni;

**12** from Dan — Ahiezer son of Ammishaddai;

**13** from Asher — Pagiel son of Ochran;

**14** from Gad — Eliasaph son of Deuel;

**15** from Naphtali — Ahira son of Enan."

**16** These were the ones chosen from the community, the leaders of their ancestral tribes — the chiefs of the clans of Israel.

**17** So Moses and Aaron took these men who had been designated by name,

**18** and on the first day of the second month they assembled the entire community. Every man twenty years old or more declared his ancestry — by clan and by ancestral house — as each was registered individually, head by head.

**19** Just as YHWH had commanded Moses, he counted them in the wilderness of Sinai.

**20** From the descendants of Reuben, Israel's firstborn — their line of descent recorded by clan and ancestral house, every male twenty years old or more who was fit for military service —

**21** the number counted from the tribe of Reuben: 46,500.

**22** From the descendants of Simeon — their line of descent recorded by clan and ancestral house, every male twenty years old or more who was fit for military service —

**23** the number counted from the tribe of Simeon: 59,300.

**24** From the descendants of Gad — their line of descent recorded by clan and ancestral house, every male twenty years old or more who was fit for military service —

**25** the number counted from the tribe of Gad: 45,650.

**26** From the descendants of Judah — their line of descent recorded by clan and ancestral house, every male twenty years old or more who was fit for military service —

**27** the number counted from the tribe of Judah: 74,600.

**28** From the descendants of Issachar — their line of descent recorded by clan and ancestral house, every male twenty years old or more who was fit for military service —

**29** the number counted from the tribe of Issachar: 54,400.

**30** From the descendants of Zebulun — their line of descent recorded by clan and ancestral house, every male twenty years old or more who was fit for military service —

**31** the number counted from the tribe of Zebulun: 57,400.

**32** From the descendants of Joseph — specifically from the descendants of Ephraim — their line of descent recorded by clan and ancestral house, every male twenty years old or more who was fit for military service —

**33** the number counted from the tribe of Ephraim: 40,500.

**34** From the descendants of Manasseh — their line of descent recorded by clan and ancestral house, every male twenty years old or more who was fit for military service —

**35** the number counted from the tribe of Manasseh: 32,200.

**36** From the descendants of Benjamin — their line of descent recorded by clan and ancestral house, every male twenty years old or more who was fit for military service —

**37** the number counted from the tribe of Benjamin: 35,400.

**38** From the descendants of Dan — their line of descent recorded by clan and ancestral house, every male twenty years old or more who was fit for military service —

**39** the number counted from the tribe of Dan: 62,700.

**40** From the descendants of Asher — their line of descent recorded by clan and ancestral house, every male twenty years old or more who was fit for military service —

**41** the number counted from the tribe of Asher: 41,500.

**42** From the descendants of Naphtali — their line of descent recorded by clan and ancestral house, every male twenty years old or more who was fit for military service —

**43** the number counted from the tribe of Naphtali: 53,400.

**44** These were the men counted by Moses and Aaron and the twelve leaders of Israel, each one representing his ancestral house.

**45** So the total of all the Sons of Israel counted by their ancestral houses — every man twenty years old or more who was fit for military service in Israel —

**46** the grand total came to 603,550.

**47** The Levites, however, were not counted along with the others by their ancestral tribe.

**48** For YHWH had spoken to Moses, saying:

**49** "The tribe of Levi alone you must not register, and you must not include them in the census of the Sons of Israel.

**50** Instead, put the Levites in charge of the Tabernacle of the Testimony — all its furnishings and everything that belongs to it. They are to carry the Tabernacle and all its equipment, and they are to take care of it, and they are to set up camp around it.

**51** Whenever the Tabernacle is to move, the Levites are to take it down, and whenever it is to be set up, the Levites are to erect it. Any outsider who comes near it is to be put to death.

**52** The Sons of Israel are to pitch their tents by divisions, each man in his own camp and under his own banner.

**53** But the Levites are to camp around the Tabernacle of the Testimony, so that wrath does not fall on the community of the Sons of Israel. The Levites are to stand guard over the Tabernacle of the Testimony."

**54** The Sons of Israel did everything exactly as YHWH had commanded Moses. That is what they did.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 1 — "in the Tent of Meeting" (באהל מועד / be'ohel mo'ed):** The Hebrew *'ohel mo'ed* literally means "tent of appointed meeting" — not merely a meeting place but a designated point of encounter between YHWH and Israel. I kept "Tent of Meeting" as this phrase has become sufficiently transparent in English. The temporal marker — "the first day of the second month, in the second year" — places this precisely one month after the Tabernacle was erected (Exod 40:17) and eleven months after the Sinai arrival. The Vaticanus LXX (p. 110) opens Numbers with a decorated heading and reads *en te eremo tou Sina* (in the wilderness of Sinai), matching the MT exactly.

**Verse 2 — "head by head" (לגלגלתם / legulgolotam):** The Hebrew *gulgolet* means "skull" — the census counts literally "by their skulls." This is the origin of the book's Latin/English name *Numeri/Numbers*. I rendered the idiom as "head by head" to preserve the concrete, individual nature of the counting while avoiding the strangeness of "by their skulls." The phrase emphasizes that every single man was personally accounted for — not estimated in groups.

**Verse 3 — "fit for military service" (כל יצא צבא / kol yotse' tsava'):** The Hebrew literally reads "everyone going out to the army" — the phrase *yotse' tsava'* describes men who "go forth" to war. I chose "fit for military service" to make the purpose of this census immediately clear: this is a military muster, not a demographic survey. The entire chapter is organized around Israel's identity as YHWH's marching army. The LXX renders this *pas ho ekporeuomenos en dynamei* (everyone going out in force), confirming the military sense.

**Verse 4 — "the head of his ancestral house" (ראש לבית אבתיו / ro'sh leveit 'avotav):** The Hebrew *ro'sh* (head) combined with *beit 'avot* (house of fathers) designates a patriarchal clan chief. These twelve men are not merely assistants — they are the recognized authorities of their tribes. I rendered "ancestral house" throughout the chapter rather than "father's house" to convey the multigenerational scope of these kinship structures.

**Verse 16 — "the chiefs of the clans of Israel" (ראשי אלפי ישראל / ra'shey 'alfey Yisra'el):** The Hebrew *'alfey* can mean both "thousands" and "clans/divisions." Most translations render "thousands," but the word likely carries the older sense of "clan" or "military unit" here. I used "clans" in this verse to preserve the sociological meaning, though the dual sense — leaders of both kinship groups and military divisions — is characteristic of Israel's organization at Sinai.

**Verses 20–43 — The census formula:** The Hebrew uses a highly formulaic repetition across twelve tribal entries. Each follows the identical pattern: *livney [tribe], toldotam lemishpehotam leveit 'avotam, bemispar shemot, kol zakhar...* ("For the sons of [tribe], their generations by their clans by their ancestral houses, by the number of names, every male..."). As a dynamic translation, I slightly condensed the formula — "their line of descent recorded by clan and ancestral house" — to maintain readability across twenty-four verses of repetition while preserving the rhythm and completeness of the listing. The repetition itself is theologically purposeful: it signals the orderly, thorough nature of YHWH's muster, treating each tribe with identical dignity.

**Verse 24 — Gad's position:** In the MT (Leningrad Codex), Gad appears third in the census list (after Reuben and Simeon), which differs from the birth order in Genesis. The Vaticanus LXX places Gad here as well, reading *tois huiois Gad*. This positioning likely reflects Gad's encampment alongside Reuben and Simeon on the south side of the Tabernacle (Num 2:10–16). The census order follows military-camp geography, not birth order.

**Verse 46 — "603,550":** This grand total has generated enormous scholarly discussion. The Hebrew *shesh me'ot 'eleph ushloshet 'alaphim wahamesh me'ot wahamishim* is unambiguous numerically. Some scholars read *'eleph* here not as "thousand" but as "clan" or "military unit," which would yield a much smaller total population. I translate the numbers at face value, as the Masoretic text intends them, while noting the interpretive question. The LXX reads identically: *hexakosioi chiliades kai trischilioi kai pentakosioi kai pentekonta*.

**Verse 47 — The Levitical exemption:** The abrupt shift — "The Levites, however, were not counted" — is striking. The Hebrew *vehaLevi'im lematteh 'avotam lo hotpaqdu betokham* sets Levi apart syntactically and theologically. They belong not to the military order but to the sacred order. I used "however" to mark the contrast clearly. This exemption is foundational for the rest of Numbers, where the Levites receive their own separate census (ch. 3–4) tied to Tabernacle service rather than warfare.

**Verse 50 — "Tabernacle of the Testimony" (משכן העדת / mishkan ha'edut):** The Hebrew *'edut* (testimony/witness) refers to the covenant tablets housed within the Ark. The Tabernacle is called "of the Testimony" because it is defined by what it contains — God's covenant witness. I retained this phrase rather than simplifying to "Tabernacle" because the specification matters: the Levites guard the covenant itself, not merely a tent.

**Verse 51 — "Any outsider who comes near it is to be put to death" (והזר הקרב יומת / wehazzar haqqarev yumat):** The Hebrew *zar* means "stranger" or "unauthorized person" — not a foreigner in the ethnic sense, but anyone not of the Levitical order. I chose "outsider" to capture this cultic-boundary meaning. The death penalty for encroachment underscores the dangerous holiness radiating from the Tabernacle; the Levites serve as a protective buffer between YHWH's consuming presence and the rest of the camp.

**Verse 53 — "so that wrath does not fall" (ולא יהיה קצף / welo' yihyeh qetseph):** The Hebrew *qetseph* (wrath/fury) is a technical term for divine anger that breaks out when sacred boundaries are violated. I translated it as "wrath" rather than softening to "anger" because the word carries theological weight — it describes the lethal consequence of unmediated contact with holiness. The Levitical cordon around the Tabernacle is not ceremonial; it is protective.

**Verse 54 — "That is what they did" (כן עשו / ken 'asu):** The chapter closes with a formulaic obedience statement that echoes the Tabernacle construction narrative in Exodus 39–40 (*wayyaas Mosheh; ka'asher tsivvah YHWH*). The doubling — "did everything exactly... that is what they did" — reinforces the theme of precise compliance. Israel at Sinai is portrayed as a people who obey completely, in sharp contrast to what will follow in Numbers 11–25.

**General note on the LXX comparison:** The Vaticanus text across pp. 110–111 closely tracks the Hebrew throughout Numbers 1. The Greek renders the census formula with equal repetition (*kata demous auton, kat' oikous patrion auton, kata arithmon onomaton auton*). The tribal numbers match the MT in every case. One minor variant: in verse 14, the MT reads "Deuel" (דעואל) while some LXX traditions read "Reuel" (Ραγουηλ), a dalet/resh confusion common in Hebrew manuscripts. The Leningrad Codex clearly reads *dalet*, and I follow it.

— PNEUMA
